Service business where delivery quality depends heavily on individual practitioner skill
claims.mechanism: When studio delivery cannot be fully mechanized (because tattoo quality is artist-dependent), the business cannot scale via cookie-cutter replication. But this same dependency creates demand among studio owners for business and operational coaching, making the gym launch model a natural fit for the industry.
bounded_by: Hormozi normally does not recommend the gym launch model; he makes an exception here because of the specific skill-dependency structure of tattooing

claims.topic: Delivery Time Crowding Out Marketing Consistency
claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a solo funding broker experiencing a revenue roller coaster (high delivery months blocking marketing, followed by revenue dips) has a delivery-crowding-marketing problem: the delivery workload consumes roughly half the founder's time, preventing consistent promotion.
conditions: founder handles both delivery and marketing
revenue volatility present (good months followed by slow months)
solo founder service business
claims.mechanism: When delivery demand peaks, marketing effort drops to near zero; when delivery slows, marketing resumes but with a lag, producing a feast-famine revenue oscillation. Systemizing or delegating delivery breaks the oscillation.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that giveaway-style attraction offers do not work for cold outbound email; giveaways perform well only in one-to-many communications such as content marketing or paid advertising, where broad reach amplifies the give.
conditions: B2B service offer
cold outbound email channel (e.g., 2,000+ emails per day)
claims.mechanism: Cold one-to-one outbound relies on perceived relevance and personal value exchange; a giveaway lacks the specificity and reciprocity signal needed to generate replies from strangers.
bounded_by: Giveaways do work for paid ads and organic content where one-to-many broadcast amplifies the offer (Hormozi, same session)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that for an operations leadership hire at the ~$5M revenue stage, LinkedIn targeted outreach to people currently in equivalent roles at similarly-sized auto businesses (or comparable trades) combined with Indeed ads will surface the most viable candidate pool; direct outreach framed as replicating their current role at a new company is the most effective pitch.
conditions: business revenue ~$5M
geographically concentrated business
hiring first operations leader

numbers.axis: outreach volume for leadership hire

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when employees say they want equity, they typically only actually want two of its four components (cash flow and sale upside); the other two components (risk and control) are unwanted. This realization enables founders to offer phantom equity structures that deliver the wanted components without transferring control or adding shared risk.
conditions: employee expresses desire for equity
claims.mechanism: Decomposing equity into four named components (cash flow, sale upside, risk, control) exposes that most employees do not actually want risk or control, allowing founders to satisfy the stated desire with non-dilutive instruments.
bounded_by: does not apply to co-founders or early investors who seek control explicitly

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in a geographically constrained (local/regional) service business, a growth-increment percentage bonus (e.g., 30% of year-over-year growth) is a poor incentive structure because local market capacity limits prevent reliable growth, discouraging and demoralizing the operator. A straight profit-share percentage of total profit is preferred for these businesses.
conditions: geographically constrained business (local/regional)
non-geographically constrained fast-growth businesses are the comparison
claims.mechanism: Local market capacity caps growth potential; tying compensation to growth rate punishes operators for market constraints outside their control, reducing motivation.

numbers.axis: growth-incentive percentage for non-geographically constrained businesses

anchors.region: example growth bonus for fast-growth unconstrained business

bounded_by: growth-increment bonus is appropriate for businesses not geographically constrained

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends reverse-engineering the profit-share percentage from the desired on-target earnings (OTE) for the role rather than selecting an arbitrary percentage. The specific percentage is irrelevant; what matters is that three compensation scenarios are defined: below target (termination risk), on target, and above target (upside).
conditions: profit-share compensation design for any role
claims.mechanism: Starting from desired OTE and working backward to percentage aligns compensation structure to role market rate rather than to an arbitrary number that may be either too dilutive or too low to motivate.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for private multi-location brick-and-mortar expansion, having a profit-share-aligned operator boots-on-the-ground at each location is the necessary condition for genuine decentralization and independent unit operation; without a profit-tied operator at each location, expansion stalls under founder hand-holding.
conditions: founder seeks to expand without proportional time increase
private multi-location brick-and-mortar business
claims.mechanism: Profit-share alignment gives the location operator incentives to make decisions autonomously, because their compensation rises or falls with location profit, removing the need to escalate to the founder.
bounded_by: Hormozi has not yet observed a private operator do exceptional multi-location expansion without a profit-tied boots-on-ground person at each store

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends running separate P&Ls per location to enable location-level profit-share calculations, even within a single-entity legal structure, as long as the financials can be calculated at the location level.
conditions: multi-location business without separate LLCs per location

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for residential concrete contractors using a cost-plus custom-bid model, the primary profit lever is pricing, not operational efficiency, because the customer never sees the cost breakdown and therefore the contractor can add margin without any visible change to the offer.
conditions: Custom project bidding
Customer does not see itemized cost breakdown
Residential concrete contracting
claims.mechanism: When customers are presented only a final project price, the price anchor is set entirely by the seller; the buyer has no reference point to evaluate whether cost-plus math is being used, so raising the output price incurs no new buyer resistance.
bounded_by: Does not apply when itemized quotes are required (e.g., government contracts, bid transparency mandates)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that emailing a quote after an in-person visit is a sales process failure; presenting the price and collecting the deposit in person on the same visit produces a close rate approximately three times higher than emailing the quote post-visit.
conditions: Deposit is collectible at visit
Prospect was already visited in person
Residential home-services or contracting sales
claims.mechanism: In-person closing maintains emotional momentum from the visit; emailed quotes introduce a cooling period and allow the prospect to solicit competing bids.

numbers.axis: Close rate by quote delivery method (residential contracting)

anchors.region: in-person close at visit with deposit
anchors.value: approximately 3x higher than email close rate

bounded_by: Does not apply where regulatory requirements mandate written bids in advance
Requires sales rep to have full pricing authority at the visit

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when evaluating whether to pursue a new customer segment alongside an existing one, the key test is whether it materially changes your day-to-day operations: if it does not, pursue it; if pursuing the new segment requires effectively starting a separate business, it becomes a cost-benefit decision calibrated to the opportunity size.
conditions: Decision involves B2C residential vs. B2B commercial or institutional
Founder is considering a second customer segment alongside an existing one
claims.mechanism: Operational overlap determines whether incremental pursuit is additive or dilutive; a segment that reuses existing delivery infrastructure is essentially free optionality.
bounded_by: Does not apply when new segment requires a structurally different delivery model or sales team

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that an insurance-company distribution deal that mandates a product to thousands of clients functions as a lead generation channel, not a guaranteed sales contract: the mandate drives inbound interest but the seller must still close each individual customer.
conditions: Clients must choose to activate purchase
Insurance company mandates product as condition of coverage
claims.mechanism: Insurance mandate removes the awareness problem but does not eliminate the sales and conversion problem; the buyer still needs to act and transact.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a company selling to high-net-worth clients where wildfire insurance is unavailable without the product faces a near-forced purchase dynamic, and this motivated-buyer context justifies a high probability-of-close assumption when modeling revenue from an insurance-mandate lead pool.
conditions: Homeowners have demonstrated willingness to buy when insurance and product are bundled
Insurance is unavailable to homeowners without the product
Market feedback confirms demand
claims.mechanism: When a product is required to access a necessary adjacent product (insurance), purchase motivation is extrinsic and urgent rather than aspirational, raising close probability materially above cold-outreach norms.
bounded_by: Applies specifically to cases where the insurance mandate is regulatory or contractual in nature
Does not apply to soft recommendations or suggestions from insurers

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a B2C residential customer base delivered via an insurance company distribution deal should be treated as high-volume B2C individual sales — each install is a separate transaction requiring a separate close — rather than as a single enterprise contract, because the installs are executed individually.
conditions: Each homeowner must individually purchase and schedule an install
Insurance company distributes mandated product list to residential homeowners
bounded_by: Does not apply if the insurance company itself is the contract counterparty and manages all install coordination
